original_abstract: Time is our most precious, nonrenewable resource, yet it is often the most neglected factor in global affairs. In the face of escalating climate change, economic instability, and violent conflict, the way we manage time frequently determines the difference between success and catastrophe. Despite its importance, an immense amount of time is currently wasted—not only our own, but more critically, the "environmental time" of nature and the "existential time" of marginalized populations. This article argues that achieving sustainable peace requires a radical shift in our temporal perspective, moving away from reactive, short-term policies toward a more adaptive "temporament." By defining the multidimensional nature of time and surveying common temporal deficiencies in modern diplomacy, this paper establishes a framework for a more responsible and effective approach to conflict transformation.
humanized_abstract: Time is humanity’s rarest and irreplaceable resource and it is probably the one that is used the least in the global arena. We are entering an era in which our time management could be what determines whether a project or policy fails or succeeds in the face of accelerating challenges such as climate change, economic crisis or armed violence. But although our time and the time of others is being wasted in the most unsustainable of ways (environmental, human existential time) it is unlikely that sustainable peace will be achieved with a different temporality based on a “temporament” more attuned to the new demands and dynamics of peace-building from crisis to sustainable peace through a transition period. This working paper examines the multidimensionality of time as understood in peace-building, and with reference to the diplomacy practices of key international players, some of the dominant temporal logics that hinder conflict transformation.
